content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 28, 2016

John Z'berg
425 Cirby Way, # 50
Roseville, CA 95678

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-16-123

Dear Mr. Z'berg:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the applicability of other laws.

QUESTIONS

1. Are the payments you made to purchase items in connection with your campaign for state office "net debt" for purposes of raising campaign funds after an election?

2. If you are permitted to raise funds after the election, are you required to use the contributions to reimburse yourself for filing fees before expenses?

CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2. No. Under the facts you describe you did not incur any debt. Payments you made outright for expenses related to your campaign, including payment of filing fees, are not considered "net debt" as defined in Section 18531.61(b)(3).

FACTS

     You ran for State Assembly in the June 7, 2016 election, but were not elected to that office. You personally paid the necessary filing fees and purchased items for your campaign. However, you did not raise funds from others of $2,000 or more and therefore your campaign never qualified as a committee under Section 82013(a).

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-123
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Section 85316 provides:

"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b) [referring to state officeholder accounts], a contribution for an election may be accepted by a candidate for elective state office after the date of the election only to the extent that the contribution does not exceed net debts outstanding from the election, and the contribution does not otherwise exceed the applicable contribution limit for that election."

Regulation 18531.61(b)(3) defines "net debts outstanding" as an amount necessary to cover the cost of raising funds, costs related to complying with certain post-election requirements and other necessary administrative costs related to winding down the campaign, legal fees and expenses incurred directly in connection with monitoring the count of absentee or provisional ballots for the election, or with a ballot recount for the election, the total amount of unpaid debts, loans and accrued expenditures incurred with respect to the election.

Under your facts, you did not loan the campaign any money, you have not incurred any debt and have no obligations outstanding after the election. You are asking whether you may raise funds after the election to reimburse yourself for the filing fees you paid for your candidacy. Payments that a candidate has made to cover costs related to the candidate's campaign are not considered debt outstanding from the election under Section 85316 or Regulation 18531.61(b)(3).

In addition, dictionary definitions of "debt" state that a debt signifies "a duty or obligation to pay money" and "something owed." The payments you made for filing fees and purchasing items for your campaign are not debt. There is nothing in your facts to indicate that you were owed these monies.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

sections.question:
I. Are the payments you made to purchase items in connection with your campaign for state 
office "net debt" for purposes of raising campaign funds after an election? 
2. If you are permitted to raise funds after the election, are you required to use the 
contributions to reimburse yourself for filing fees before expenses?
sections.conclusion:
1. and 2. No. Under the facts you describe you did not incur any debt. Payments you made 
outright for expenses related to your campaign, including payment of filing fees, are not considered 
"net debt" as defined in Section 18531.61 (b )(3 ).

sections.analysis:
Section 85316 provides:  
"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b) [referring to state officeholder accounts], a 
contribution for an election may be accepted by a candidate for elective state office 
after the date of the election only to the extent that the contribution does not exceed 
net debts outstanding from the election, and the contribution does not otherwise 
exceed the applicable contribution limit for that election." 
Regulation 18531.61 (b )(3) defines "net debts outstanding" as an amount necessary to cover 
the cost of raising funds, costs related to complying with certain post-election requirements and 
other necessary administrative costs related to winding down the campaign, legal fees and expenses 
incurred directly in connection with monitoring the count of absentee or provisional ballots for the 
election, or with a ballot recount for the election, the total amount of unpaid debts, loans and 
accrued expenditures incurred with respect to the election. 
Under your facts, you did not loan the campaign any money, you have not incurred any debt 
and have no obligations outstanding after the election. You are asking whether you may raise funds 
after the electiqn to reimburse yourself for the filing fees you paid for your candidacy. Payments 
that a candidate has made to cover costs related to the candidate's campaign are not considered debt 
outstanding from the election under Section 85316 or Regulation 18531.61 (b )(3 ). 
In addition, dictionary definitions of "debt" state that a debt signifies "a duty or obligation to 
pay money" and "something owed." The payments you made for filing fees and purchasing items 
for your campaign are not debt. There is nothing in your facts to indicate that you were owed these 
monies.
